speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is George Gottlieb. It is September 24th, 1996, tape four. Mr. Gottlieb, we were talking about Auschwitz-Birkenau Tell me about what you saw on a daily basis, the routine of the camp, other than the food that you've described already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, at the very beginning the first week, we had just nothing to do. Lingered around and found out where we are and what's happening there. We found out that Auschwitz and Birkenau, both of-- both of them, we called it just the camp, it was an extermination camp. And the word extermination, I think so, should be used the same way as they are killing bugs. The purpose of the camp was, we already learned, was the famous Nazi words of Final Solution. And super race, et cetera, that who they are and we are. We are the scum of the Earth. And after a week we were selected to go to work. A lot of people went, and a lot of people worked in quarries. Some people worked in different-- different mines, different chemical plants. My brother and I, thankfully we were selected to go to work, what they called the Canadish group, the Canadian group. Our work was to go to the railroad station. And we were those famous guys who were yelling and screaming to the incoming trains, to get out, and form the line of five. And our job was to remove everything from the train, everything what the people brought in. I sort them, different piles, what we had to sort, children's clothes, men's clothes, ladies clothes, books. I mean, shoes. You can understand totally, sort everything. And basically get rid of anything which was perishable. They didn't want it should rot there, and get rid of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also, at the same time while we opened, we had real valuables. You know? Like, it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's amazing, that if you think about it, that people coming-- or going into a ghetto, they brought silverware with them. So that silverware came to Auschwitz-Birkenau. They had jewelry. They had necklaces and et cetera, which was hidden in pockets. They had money. I mean, they have all kind of different thing, what basically we were not allowed to touch. That was something what they had a box for smaller jewelry, another one for bigger one. And we had to pile the clothes and suitcases, shoes, everything separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the jewelry? Who took that? Who was in charge of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all SS. We were on the-- on-- continuously under SS guard. That you have to understand, when we left camp to the railroad station, we were strip searched, That nothing should come out of the camp, come back to camp, once again, strip searched. That nothing can come from the radio station, back. That's why we couldn't manipulate, we couldn't organize anything. Always, the same SS guard who took us. It was a 12-hour shift. The shift was we had to be at the station at 7:00. We stayed till 7:00 at night. I was always with my brother, by the way, together, always on a day shift while we did this work. But we found out the whole operation. We saw-- I had the unpleasant thing of seeing Dr. Mengele every day. Suddenly we knew when we saw the big chimneys, and we know the chimneys is basically, that-- that-- the-- the crematorium. And we saw where the gas chamber is. And also what we have seen, where the smell comes from. The smell came that even with the tremendous German precision-- while they were precise in everything, what they did, they did not calculate it properly, that it took somewhere in around 40 minutes to burn the bodies. And the gas chamber took 10 to 11 minutes, to kill the people. So the crematoriums could not supply fast enough to get rid of the bodies. So they were digging huge ditches. And those ditches were those with gasoline, and just put on fire, outside. And that was an unbelievable smell. And that was an unbelievable knowledge, that that's happening. I mean, once you are that close to it, we were so close to what's happening there that the mental and-- and-- and-- the physical was nothing. Physically, it was the best job available. There was no-- no not-- no better job, for the simple reason, although we were forbidden, [COUGHS] then, well, you are on a diet, what I told you before. And then you see food coming in from ghettos. First of all, you know the food comes from a-- from a another Jewish family. You stuff your mouth, even if-- even if the punishment is harsh. Punishment could have been, at that time, a shot. But you stuffed your mouth and you ate. So basically, that was the best job available. Also, that once we noticed that the jewelry is going into the smaller box, the smaller jewelry, bigger jewelry, one day the SS-- the same SS guard who was a pleasant-looking fellow, he was a German SS. By the way, we were-- there you had a lot of Ukrainian and Lithuanian SS, also in Auschwitz. And this guy was pleasant. I mean, he basically never did anything. He yelled, screamed. But he was never rough to us. I was brash, young, and not thinking. I grabbed like, a handful of good jewelry. But I thought it was a good jewelry and just put it in his hand. So he looked at me first. And his reaction was it goes to the box. And I just picked my shoulders up like, here you are. And I turned away. And my brother saw that he turned away and put it in his pocket. So when we saw that he put the jewelry in the pocket, it was a-- quite a good feeling. Our feeling was that OK, he's a-- he's a comrade in crime.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm stealing, he's taking. I had no use for it. To me, it could have been diamonds or rubies, or God knows what. It has-- what's the difference? I let him have it. What's the difference who has it? I prefer that he should have it than the German Reich should have it, that the government should have it. Maybe he can do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Living with the rumors in camp, even after seven o'clock when we went back, we-- we were counted again on top of the appel. They knew that we left from that camp exactly 100. So 100 had to come back. And the SS, they interior guard had to count. Not only the-- the guard was waiting and taking us. So the rumors were, basically, that through the wires you were yelling and screaming continuously. Our yell and scream was try to find out if mom is there. We knew that right next to us there was at another man's camp. But the camp right next to it was a ladies camp. You know, like, in row there were many, many camps. And one day we got the-- the unbelievably relieved news by saying through the rumors that yes, mom is alive. Naturally, the same rumor brought it back, most probably to her, that we are alive. Naturally, she had no idea who is the we. We knew by having our job, the so-called Canadish group, the Canadian group, that dad and grandpa immediately went to gas chamber and crematorium. At least we hope it was the gas chamber and crematorium. But anyway, we knew exactly they went-- they went on the wrong side of the track.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our aim, our duty, our what-- desire to live was to find out if mom is alive, how is she, and what she's doing. And when we found this one up-- and as a matter, a couple of days later, once again through this rumor mill, even the barrack number was given to us, where she is. She was in barrack 14. And shamelessly, I went over to the SS guard, the same SS guard who-- who was quite nice. And I asked him the question, is there any way we can let mom know that we are alive and what-how and what? And he just turned around, kicked me in the pants and told me to get back in-- into the line. I got a healthy kick from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And didn't expect too much more. Really didn't expect any miracle from him. And a couple of days later, maybe a week later, on the morning when we were leaving, he selected out of the 100, 10 people, which included my brother and I. And there was a pushcart there. You know, with the two big wheels and with some blankets, or some clothing on top of it. And he started to tell us oh, that SS guard took the rest of the 90 of them away to work. And he took the 10 of us. We pushed that cart. And we had no idea where we are going, what are we doing. And suddenly with the pushcart, suddenly he stopped in front of the ladies' camp. He handed some documents over to the guard. And the gate opened up and we went in. And in barrack 14, we stopped-- in front of barrack 14, he stopped and handed me and my brother a brown paper bag. The paper bag had full bread in it, German bread, what they were eating, not our garbage. It must have been a pound or a kilo, I don't know how much butter, and sausage, and a pocket knife. He looked at his wife. She said, you have 20 minutes. Find your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. We were looking and suddenly we heard mom's voice. Mom was a beautiful lady. And suddenly, you see somebody running to you, bald with a long striped gown. We didn't recognize mom. [CRYING] We gave her the goodies. We kissed. And the 20 minutes were gone. And that's the last time I saw mom. It's a tough sight. And a tougher memory. A beautiful, beautiful woman, looking like that. Until today, I hear her saying, be proud of your heritage. And the last words, be proud of your name. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope we can be.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So here is an SS who volunteered to be Hitler's guard and had a human touch. That's the last time I saw mom. That's the last time that we saw mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then we knew that she's alive. And we knew that she's going to be strong. That was her demand to us. And basically, that's what kept us-- kept us fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the guard's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never heard a name there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Basic idea is that if you ask how-- what made you fight? What made you survive? Is the very, very, very few conversation between the two brothers, my brother and I, is we said to each other that we must survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because we are the men. And we have to take care of mom. We knew that dad is gone. Grandpa-- is naturally gone. And we knew that she's alive. That gave us the strength to fight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That job came to the end in Auschwitz, sometimes in August. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suddenly we were-- by the way, that I would like to, just for the record, to see that once you lived in Birkenau or Auschwitz, it didn't mean if you are in camp that you are alive and you are going to survive. It could happen during the day or at night. If not enough transport came in, that mean not enough new people came in and there was room in the gas chambers and crematoriums, automatically a truck or two came in, loaded up people, took us away, took people away. We learned that immediately, that that was a death sentence. Anytime during the day, or at night, or after appel, suddenly, a truck pulled up. And the truck was basically full of SS, surrounded, x people, march. That was their death, marched straight to the crematorium. That was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gottlieb, of reflecting upon what you observed during Auschwitz, I'd like to ask you about your reaction to seeing what you saw in the-- in the-- in the sense of the-- the bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no reaction. There is-- it's-- it's-- you became not only a number, but not-normal human being who just strived for one thing, is waking up tomorrow morning, of-- of-- of-- of being alive and being to survive and waiting for the-- for the miracle to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I don't think so. There was very little thought, very little-- I mean, some people ask me the question, what was your-- you know, like, you are starved months, and over a year goes by. And what was your-- your dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My dream was-- as a kid, I love to-- even today I'm a-- I love chocolate. But my dream was to have a slice of bread with butter and put on thickly, sugar on it. I never had that before. I never had bread and sugar. I never had bread, butter, and sugar on top of it. But probably that's what the body missed the most. That was my dream. My dream wasn't to have a-- a decent meal, or it wasn't to have-- to have a meal, what mom-- mom used to cook for us. It was a piece of bread-- a piece of bread with butter and sugar on it. That was my dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you also, about violence that you observed, if any? What violence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely none. I have never seen-- yes, there was some pushing, some you know, going in the line for soup or go-- standing in line for the bread. Because you could see that the kapo, when he sliced the bread, there was a thicker one bread than the other one. People tried to push.

speaker: interviewer
text: But absolute violence? Never. I never-- in between people-- now, you are asking me about the camp people, not talking about outside violence? You are not talking-- I-- I-- I'm talking-- no. What I'm talking about is the violence that you saw directed from the officers to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then I can talk about personal experience. Personal experience was later in camp, where-- what you have to understand, we had only one punishment in camp. And the punishment was a bullet. We were fully aware of it, that there is no such thing that they are going to put you in a worse camp, or in a worse position. I mean, they-- they you didn't obey something, they shoot at you without any warning or without any questions, without any problem. The shooting was every-day affair. Every day you heard-- a the gun, the machine gun going on. It-- it-- it wasn't news. And one day when I-- when I-- when I come to that camp, maybe I should describe exactly what was the reason why I got-- go into the personal--

speaker: interviewer
text: You make mention previously about the [GERMAN]-- the-- the-- am I saying it correctly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the twin camp, the [GERMAN] camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you observe? You said you were in eye-shot range. What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was that-- not-- not only what we see. What we saw, what what we heard it. It was the moaning and the groaning and the yelling and the screaming and the begging. And it's-- it's-- it's little boys or little girls, or grown-up people, old people, regardless who-- who was basically there. And through-- they had-- they had barracks which had windows. We didn't have any windows. They had barracks with windows. And-- and-- and-- and we thought from tearing at each other and yelling at each other. And we saw that-- we heard the tremendous-- I mean, the pain, what these people had to go through, that must have been beyond any human pain. Beyond what-- what they-- And we have seen, sometimes, outside, people walking with-- naturally they didn't have any gauze or any-- or and sterile way of doing it. But people half naked, walking-- walking around with a rag or something, covering up their wound. And some of them collapsing, some of them, many of them, going against the wire. I mean, that was probably the-- the-- the-- the most difficult part of-- of Birkenau to-- to be able-- to be next door to-- to be next to that [GERMAN] camp, to-- to see what can be done. And-- and once again, you know, the only separation is six, seven, or eight-foot area in between the wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could talk. You could hear. You could-- you could-- you could communicate with those who were still in a-- in a-- you know, sat in a position to be able to communicate. It was-- it was one of the worst part of any memory of camp. What happened? When did you leave Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, sometimes in-- in the month of August. Suddenly we were pushed on a train. By the way, that's the first time we went in a passenger train. And we had no idea where we are going. And that train went on for-- and again, two or three days. And finally, we arrived to a-- in Germany. We new that we are heading and we passed on near Berlin. I mean, well, geographic, we knew enough where we are. And we are just north of Berlin, northeast of Berlin, to a city which called Braunschweig if you want to spell, it's B-R-A-U-N-S-C-H-W-E-I-G. Braunschweig is a comparatively large town where they had Bussing [GERMAN]. Bussing truck manufacturing plant. Bussing is B-U-S-S-I-N-G. Bussing, to the best of my knowledge, was or is a division of Mercedes Benz Truck Division. And there we-- the train stopped. And probably a mile from the train station, they had a big camp, big, big camp. Labor camp. This was strictly labor camp. This was not an extermination camp. There was no Final Solution or extermination here. This was death by-- by-- by labor. This camp, to compare to Birkenau, was a luxury camp. Because we had triple-decker bunks. It didn't mean that you slept in a bank-- or in a bunk. There was eight people, I mean, in a row, on a wooden plank. And on the wood sometimes, they had a little bit-- so there was a little straw there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived to this camp. And immediately, the next morning, we started to go to work where the work was that we had to go from the railroad station to the factory. We carried from steel to wood, to any material whatsoever that was necessary in fabrication of the-- of the trucks. And who is that we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was with me. But all the other prisoners who worked. But my brother and I were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you selected to-- to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. Just x people, x person, or x amount of person were going from-- from-- from our camp. And they-- I don't believe-- maybe they went by the ability of who were able to work. You know, who were younger, who were physically still strong enough. I assume, but nobody told us anything, what, where, and why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was your stay at Auschwitz-Birkenau? How long altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there from basically May to August, the beginning of May to August. So it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: --four months of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you're in Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we went to Braunschweig. And Braunschweig was extremely hard labor, extremely hard. I mean, the whole day outside. And fall came. Winter came. And it was miserable. Miserable colds, miserable-- miserable conditions. Food is exactly the same as in Auschwitz. And we had a-- a-- I found out that it was a-- a criminal, but it was-- we had German kapos only there, German kapos. And they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have in-- in Auschwitz? Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gypsies or Jews. We had all kind of-- not German. We had all kind, political prisoners, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: As kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of kapos. But here we had absolute criminals, absolute criminals. I mean, the kapo who was charge in our-- in our barrack, he looked like the weightlifter. I mean, he looked like a specimen of health. And he had the biggest pleasure of kicking and punching, and-- and-- and-- and-- and any kind of brutality, whatever he could do to anybody. And you know, the-- the [GERMAN], which means Jew pig, that was the greeting of hello, I love you. That was the nicest, what he could say. But besides of-- of us, on the other side of the railroad track, there was a huge POW camp. Well, the POW camp had French, English, and Russian soldiers. Not one American uniform that we have seen. They were working inside, in the factory. We were never allowed into the factory. So the only contact, what we had, that we took from the railroad to the factory, anything, whatever had to be brought there. By the way, no mechanized equipment. Everything is physical. And they are the one who brought it in. And they worked us as-- we as human machines. So that was our only contact with them. But there are the-- once we started, and we started to work there, our-- what you have to understand, that we went through, already, four, three, four, five, six months, without any kind of whatsoever what we know hygiene, or what-- what-- what-- what existed to-- to wash your face, wash your clothes. There's no such a thing. It's the same clothes, the same shoes, the same shirts and the same pants and jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this-- you're referring to Braunschweig?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over. From-- from day one in Auschwitz, there is nothing else but a latrine. And latrine and no such a thing as toilet paper. No such thing as a soap or water or toothbrush or toothpaste or comb, or-- comb wasn't necessarily real. Because the basic idea was that once a month, they kept your hair on a brush-cut basis. But in the middle of your head, with that hand shear, they cut approximately, whatever it is, a width around two inches, a stripe. But once a month was stripe down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the basic idea was that they figured if you are such a genius and with your striped suit you can escape-- and most of the people with the tattoo on the arm, at least on your head if you take off your hat, or whatever it is, it's quite obvious that you are a concentration-- and they called it a [NON-ENGLISH] prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, our work was, there, not only hard. But an awful lot of people fell. We started off to be 28,000 at the Braunschweig. And with a minimum of food and a tremendous amount of work, and difficulty of the temperature and the rest of it, what was hard work, real hard work, one day, while we are marching for our famous lunch, I notice that the SS is feeding the German shepherds with potato peels. And that smell and the fume came up. And it was just an unbelievable temptation, let's call it that way. You know, you're hungry. I broke line. And I jumped into the place where the shepherds were feeding. And I took away a dish of potato peels.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was hollering and screaming at me. And you know, that-- the desperation came to the point of what can happen? They can kill me or I have potato peels. So maybe I'm-- maybe I'm going to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, probably, stuffed my mouth with an awful lot of potato peels. And I tossed that dish towards my brother when the SS guard, for unknown reason, instead of using his gun he used the butt of his rifle, and hit me in the back, two or three times and asked me to spit it out. Naturally, I did not spit out. I swallowed as much as I could. I found out way later, that the time when he hit me, he broke one of my vertebrae. But there is no such thing as being sick and no such a thing as not being able to do. Because if you stayed sick the next-- next day you didn't live. But the people in our group, et cetera, they knew that I can't lift. Once it was already up on the shoulder I could walk. But bending down and lift, for a couple of weeks it was hard. And the camaraderie was unbelievable. And not only camaraderie. Everybody knew if you have to help somebody, you help somebody. But by there already, many, many times. I mean, it was a-- practically daily affair, that when from your group of six, sometimes somebody didn't move. And they supposed to turn. Or somebody ordered to turn. And just the person was dead. And bodies started to pile, and-- and respect-- or I would say that not respect, because we respected life. But you know, it was an accepted fact that people die, that people cannot take it. And people are getting to the point where physically, mentally, but mostly physically, there they-- here we had only barbed wire. We had no electrical wire around it. But it wasn't required. The march was long. And I have to say that every-- that march of going from the camp to the railroad station, we went through one part of town. Until today, if somebody is telling you that they didn't know about it, somehow I asked the question that if you see 10,000 or 6,000 or 8,000-- I don't know how many at the same time-- marched with stripes and basically, feeble looking or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --funny-looking people go through, don't you ask the questions, who are they? Are they prisoners? What do you mean a prisoner? Prisoners for what? I mean, they didn't know about concentration camps. Don't tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Braunschweig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Braunschweig ended, basically, on a-- on an interesting note of-- started to have air raids in March, twice a day, 10 o'clock, 4 o'clock. It was like a routine. Till one day, finally-- by the way, we were not allowed to go to any shelter. We had to dig a ditch outside of the factory, in between the railroad station and the factory. We had a long ditch where we had to-- that was our shelter, or whatever you want to call it. Both end of the ditch there was a SS guard. Finally, after a week or 10 days of continuous air raids, finally the day come when the Allies-- the planes came in and in the morning the--
